name: Materiable
short_description: A pin-based shape-changing display that renders dynamic material properties such as flexibility, elasticity, and viscosity in response to a user's direct touch.
long_description: Materiable is a research prototype from MIT Media Lab's Tangible Media Group, presented at CHI 2016, that uses a pin-based shape display to make digital data feel like physical material. Rather than rendering only static shapes, it runs physics algorithms (including shallow-water equations for liquids and spring models for deformable solids) so that pressing on the surface elicits material-like responses, letting people perceive flexibility, elasticity, and viscosity through touch. As physical AI / interaction design, it matters because it extends shape-changing interfaces from shape alone toward rich, tangible material sensation, advancing how computational surfaces can communicate haptic and dynamic qualities to the body.
ai_note: The system is driven by hand-authored physics simulations (shallow-water equations and spring models) on a motorized pin display, with no AI or machine learning involved.

images.caption: A user presses into the Materiable pin-based shape display, which responds with liquid- and material-like deformation rendered by physics algorithms.
images.alt_text: A hand pressing down on a grid of motorized white pins that form a deformable surface, with the pins around the touch point lowered to mimic a soft, yielding material.

sources.title: Materiable
sources.publisher: MIT Media Lab Tangible Media Group

sources.title: Materiable: Rendering Dynamic Material Properties in Response to Direct Physical Touch with Shape Changing Interfaces
sources.publisher: Proceedings of the 2016 CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems (ACM)

interactions.title: Press a spot and watch it ripple like water
interactions.description: Push a finger into the pin surface and the dimple radiates outward in concentric waves, the pins rising and falling as the system runs shallow-water equations to make the display behave like a pool of liquid.

interactions.title: Feel rubber, water, and sand from the same pins
interactions.description: The motorized pins are programmed with different material models so the surface can mimic the springy push-back of rubber, the flow of water, or the crumbling give of sand, all from one display.

interactions.title: Push a deformable solid that springs back
interactions.description: Press into a region tuned as an elastic solid and the pins yield under your hand, then recover toward their resting height when you let go, conveying flexibility and elasticity through touch rather than just a static shape.

interactions.title: Sense viscosity as the surface resists you
interactions.description: Drag or press across a region authored with high viscosity and the pins move sluggishly, building a perceptive sense of a thick, slow material in the hand even without precise force-feedback motors.

interactions.title: Mock up and sculpt a landscape by hand
interactions.description: Designers use the responsive surface to prototype landscape and terrain concepts, shaping the pin field directly and watching material-like behavior instead of editing a screen model.

interactions.title: Stage an earthquake or tsunami you can touch
interactions.description: The display can run dynamic scenarios such as earthquakes and tsunamis, letting a person feel the ground heave and waves propagate across the pins as a physical, embodied simulation.

interactions.title: Knead virtual clay before you 3D print it
interactions.description: A maker pushes and smooths a region tuned as soft, plastic material to rough out a form by hand, using the same spring-and-flow physics to give digital clay a yielding tactile feel ahead of fabrication.

interactions.title: Feel a material-science lesson under your fingers
interactions.description: A student presses the same patch of surface as the instructor dials it from rigid to elastic to viscous, comparing how each behaves by touch to build intuition for material properties.
